title: St. Joshua Son of Nun (Monthly) — St. David of Thessalonica & St. Antion the Martyr
narrative:
The Monthly Commemoration of St. Joshua the Son of Nun the Commander of Israel, St. David the Dendrite of Thessalonica the Confessor-Monk, and St. Antion the Martyr

On the twenty-sixth of Sene, the holy Church celebrates the monthly commemoration of the holy Joshua the Son of Nun, the successor of Moses, the commander who led Israel across the Jordan and into the Promised Land, the man whose very name — Yehoshua, 'God saves,' the Hebrew original of the name Jesus — is the tradition's first great sign that the whole of the Old Testament is a preparation for the New. Joshua's name is the name of the Saviour, and the tradition has always read this as intentional: the man who led Israel across the Jordan into Canaan is the type of the one who would lead humanity across the waters of baptism into the Kingdom of God, the commander who conquered the Promised Land is the type of the one who would conquer death and open the doors of Paradise, the leader who circumcised Israel at Gilgal after the crossing is the type of the one who would institute the circumcision of the heart that Paul describes in his letter to the Romans.

The theology of Joshua in the Tewahedo tradition is a theology of the inherited mission: Joshua did not choose his vocation but received it from Moses, who received it from God, and the greatness of Joshua's life lies not in his originality but in his fidelity — his willingness to carry forward what had been given to him, to complete what the man before him had begun, to lead the people to the destination that Moses could see from Pisgah but not enter. The tradition honours this inherited faithfulness as deeply as it honours the more obviously dramatic vocations of prophets and martyrs: there is a greatness in being the one who finishes the work that someone greater began, in being Elisha to another's Elijah, Joshua to another's Moses, and the monthly commemoration of Joshua is the calendar's acknowledgment that the tradition of faith is always handed on and always received, that the greatness of the recipient is measured by the faithfulness with which the gift is passed forward to those who come after.

Saint David the Dendrite of Thessalonica — David 'of the trees' — is one of the most remarkable and most distinctive of the Eastern ascetics: a monk of Thessalonica who spent three years living in an almond tree outside his monastery, exposed to the elements, fasting with extraordinary severity, and from this unusual perch receiving the visits of the faithful who came to seek his counsel and his blessing. David is the tradition's dendrite — tree-dweller — in the same category as the stylites are pillar-dwellers: a man who understood that the ascetic life requires not merely fasting and vigil but a deliberate and visible exposure of the body to the conditions that the spiritual life requires of the soul, and who chose the almond tree as his pillar not because it was comfortable but because it was uncomfortable and therefore honest. His eventual descent from the tree was commanded by a vision of Christ and the apostles, who told him his time on the tree was complete and that he should go to Constantinople and strengthen the emperor in his faith; David obeyed, made the journey, and returned to Thessalonica to die in the monastery where he had begun.

Saint Antion the Martyr completes this twenty-sixth day of Sene as a witness of the great persecutions whose faithful death for the faith is preserved by the tradition as the testimony that every generation and every geography has its own Joshuas — its own people who cross the Jordan into the land that death cannot touch, who complete the journey that their predecessors began, who pass on to those who come after them an inheritance that no persecutor can confiscate. Antion's name — from the Latin Antonius, common throughout the Roman world — suggests the ordinariness of the martyr: not a bishop or a philosopher or a soldier but a man with an ordinary Roman name who in ordinary circumstances encountered the extraordinary demand that the faith sometimes makes and met it fully. The twenty-sixth day of Sene gathers the great commander of the crossing, the monk who lived in a tree, and the man with the common name who gave everything he had. Amen.
